stories.headline: Iran war escalates as gas field attacks roil global energy markets
stories.body: The war in the Middle East intensified as Israel struck Iran's massive South Pars gas field, triggering Iranian retaliatory attacks on Qatar's Ras Laffan complex and other Gulf energy infrastructure. Iran's foreign minister warned of "zero restraint" if energy sites are targeted again, while Qatar revealed nearly a fifth of its LNG export capacity has been knocked out, potentially for years. Oil prices surged past $116 per barrel and gas prices jumped 24%. The Pentagon requested an additional $200 billion from Congress to fund the war, with Defense Secretary Hegseth saying there is no "timeframe" for ending the conflict. European nations, Japan, and Gulf states scrambled to respond as the Strait of Hormuz remained effectively blocked, with Middle East oil exports down 61% in recent weeks. Russia has emerged as an unexpected beneficiary, with crude sales to India jumping 50%, while China appears relatively insulated thanks to massive strategic reserves built up over years of preparation.

stories.headline: EU summit seeks de-escalation while resisting pressure to join Iran conflict
stories.body: EU leaders at a Brussels summit called for a moratorium on attacks against energy infrastructure and pushed for a diplomatic solution to the Iran conflict, while carefully avoiding a direct confrontation with Washington. The summit conclusions urged respect for international law by "all parties" without naming the US or Israel. A separate statement signed by France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, the UK, and Japan expressed readiness to contribute to safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, but only after hostilities cease. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz was blunt: "We will only get involved when the weapons fall silent." Spain's Pedro Sanchez, the most vocal European critic of the war, said the majority of leaders had moved toward his anti-war position, while insisting Europe should not send military vessels while fighting continues.

stories.headline: Trump mocks Japan about Pearl Harbor during meeting with PM Takaichi
stories.body: President Trump drew gasps during an Oval Office meeting with Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi when, asked why he did not inform allies before attacking Iran, he invoked Japan's 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor. "Who knows better about surprise than Japan?" Trump said, before adding: "Why didn't you tell me about Pearl Harbor?" Takaichi's eyes widened visibly as laughter in the room died away. The exchange came amid broader tensions over allied participation in the Iran conflict, with Trump repeatedly complaining that allies including Japan have not helped secure the Strait of Hormuz. Takaichi later told reporters the two agreed the strait's safety was of "utmost importance" but explained the legal constraints on Japan's Self-Defense Forces under its pacifist constitution.

stories.headline: Orban blocks EU's 90 billion euro Ukraine loan in act EU leaders call 'betrayal'
stories.body: Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban refused to release a 90 billion euro loan for Ukraine that EU leaders had agreed to in December, prompting furious reactions at the Brussels summit. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz called it "a gross act of disloyalty" that would "leave deep marks," while Finland's PM said Orban was using Ukraine "as a weapon" in his election campaign ahead of Hungary's April 12 vote. European Council President Antonio Costa said "nobody can blackmail the European institutions," and Commission President Ursula von der Leyen vowed to deliver the funds "one way or the other." Orban has cited a dispute with Kyiv over a damaged oil pipeline as his reason for reneging on the deal, which had been structured to exempt Hungary, Slovakia, and the Czech Republic from contributing.

stories.headline: Denmark planned to destroy Greenland runways in case of US invasion
stories.body: Danish soldiers deployed to Greenland in January were prepared to blow up key airport runways over fears that President Trump could invade the Arctic island, according to a report by Denmark's public broadcaster DR citing 12 sources at the top of the Danish government and military. Blood supplies were also brought in to treat potential wounded. The contingency planning escalated after US special forces seized Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro in a lightning operation in Caracas on January 3, with a high-ranking Danish security source saying they "had to take all scenarios seriously." A multinational force of Danish, French, German, Norwegian, and Swedish soldiers was subsequently flown to Greenland under the cover of military exercises called Operation Arctic Endurance.

stories.headline: Cesar Chavez monuments removed across US after sexual abuse allegations
stories.body: Cities and institutions across the United States are rapidly removing monuments, renaming streets, and covering murals honoring labor icon Cesar Chavez after explosive sexual abuse allegations surfaced against him. Labor rights activist Dolores Huerta revealed she was among women and girls who say they were sexually abused by Chavez while he led the United Farm Workers union. Denver removed a bust from Cesar E. Chavez Park and will rename Cesar Chavez Day to "Si, Se Puede Day." California State University Fresno covered its Chavez statue, and Santa Ana College covered a mural featuring him. California Governor Gavin Newsom expressed support for renaming the state holiday. The revelations have shattered the legacy of one of the most celebrated figures in Latino civil rights history.

stories.headline: Judge rules RFK Jr. overstepped authority on transgender health care declaration
stories.body: A federal judge in Oregon ruled that Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. overreached when he issued a declaration calling puberty blockers and surgeries unsafe for young people experiencing gender dysphoria, finding he had not followed proper administrative procedures. The judge also denied the government's motion to dismiss the case. The ruling is the second major legal setback for Kennedy this week, after another federal judge in Boston temporarily blocked several of his vaccine policy changes. A coalition of 19 states and the District of Columbia had sued HHS over the declaration, which warned doctors they could be excluded from federal health programs if they provide gender-affirming treatments. New York Attorney General Letitia James called the ruling a needed clarity that "health care services for transgender young people remain legal."

stories.headline: Nashville Spanish-language reporter released from ICE detention after two weeks
stories.body: Estefany Rodriguez Florez, a reporter for Nashville Noticias who had done stories critical of ICE, was released from immigration detention after more than two weeks in custody, following payment of a $10,000 bond set by an immigration judge. Rodriguez, a Colombian citizen with a valid work permit and a pending asylum application, was arrested during a traffic stop on March 4 and held in facilities in Alabama and Louisiana. Her attorneys argue she was targeted because her reporting has been critical of ICE's practices under the Trump administration, calling the arrest a violation of her First and Fifth Amendment rights. The government has denied any such violation, and her lawyers plan to continue pursuing a wrongful detention case.

stories.headline: Extreme weather grips US with record March heat wave in West and blizzards in East
stories.body: More than half the United States faced extreme weather as a prolonged and unusual March heat wave baked the West while blizzards and severe storms battered eastern states. San Francisco recorded its hottest March in at least two decades with temperatures nearing 90 degrees Fahrenheit, while Phoenix was expected to top 100 degrees, something it has never done before March 26. Las Vegas was on track for its hottest March stretch ever recorded. Meanwhile, heavy snow and high winds hit parts of the Midwest and Northeast, with threats of thunderstorms and tornadoes. The MLB Cactus League in Arizona was forced to alter its schedule due to triple-digit temperatures.

stories.headline: Trump gold coin approved by hand-picked federal arts commission
stories.body: The US Commission of Fine Arts, whose members were all appointed by President Trump, approved a 24-karat gold coin bearing his likeness. The coin, which would be struck by the US Mint, was approved during a commission meeting that drew criticism from opponents who called it an unprecedented act of self-promotion by a sitting president. The commission, which advises on the design of coins, medals, and monuments, voted unanimously in favor of the design.

stories.headline: Sanchez shelves budget indefinitely, citing Iran war as overriding priority
stories.body: Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez indefinitely postponed the presentation of Spain's 2026 budget, citing the economic fallout from the Iran war after months of promising the budget would be presented this quarter. With gas prices up 24% and oil at $116 per barrel, Sanchez said the government's immediate focus is a royal decree of emergency measures to cushion citizens from energy price surges. Vice President Maria Jesus Montero, who will step down to run as the PSOE candidate in the Andalusian elections, suggested budgets might be revisited in weeks if the war ends quickly but conceded it may not happen until 2027. Opposition leader Alberto Nunez Feijoo called the delay "a lack of respect," noting the government has not presented a single budget this legislature, with the last approved accounts dating to 2023.

stories.headline: Vox internal war reaches the courts as two senior officials charged
stories.body: The bitter power struggle inside Spain's far-right Vox party has spilled into the courts, with two senior officials from party leader Santiago Abascal's inner circle charged with illegally leaking confidential information to discredit dissident former parliamentary spokesman Ivan Espinosa de los Monteros. Juan de Dios Davila and Cristina Navarro, who ran Vox's internal ethics compliance unit, allegedly leaked a confidential investigation into Espinosa to the media. The infighting intensified after Vox's disappointing results in last Sunday's Castilla y Leon elections. Meanwhile, leaked internal emails revealed that current secretary general Ignacio Garriga had accused former secretary general Javier Ortega Smith of seeking psychiatric treatment for the Espinosa-Monasterio couple, exposing deep fractures in the party leadership.

stories.headline: Rufian and Montero explore new left-wing alliance for next general elections
stories.body: Former ERC spokesman Gabriel Rufian and Podemos co-founder Irene Montero are exploring the creation of a new left-wing electoral front ahead of Spain's next general elections, planning a joint public event in Barcelona. The initiative aims to unite fragmented left-wing forces outside the PSOE, coming at a time when both Sumar and Podemos face questions about their relevance and electoral viability. The move has generated significant attention across the Spanish political landscape, with multiple outlets covering the potential realignment of the left.

stories.headline: Spanish royals meet Pope Leon XIV at Vatican ahead of papal visit to Spain
stories.body: King Felipe VI and Queen Letizia traveled to the Vatican for their first formal audience with Pope Leon XIV, who was elected in May 2025. The meeting is laying the groundwork for the Pope's apostolic visit to Spain from June 6-12, with stops planned in Barcelona, Madrid, and the Canary Islands. It will be the first papal visit to Spain since 2011, as Pope Francis had avoided major Western countries during his 12-year pontificate. The visit comes against the backdrop of a stalled agreement between the Spanish government and the Catholic Church on compensation for abuse victims, with the Spanish bishops' conference having just postponed the signing of the protocol.

stories.headline: Valencia's Fallas end with anti-war themed Nit de la Crema
stories.body: Valencia's Fallas festival concluded with the traditional Nit de la Crema, burning more than 700 monuments across the city in a celebration that carried a strong pacifist message amid the ongoing Middle East conflict. The centerpiece municipal falla featured a giant figure of Charlie Chaplin with a rifle over his shoulder gazing at a butterfly, an anti-war allegory that resonated throughout the festival. The 2026 edition, marking a decade since Fallas was declared UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage, drew massive crowds with favorable weather after two rain-disrupted years, and passed without major incidents despite growing tourist numbers.

stories.headline: Teachers and doctors plan joint mass protest at Catalan Parliament on Friday
stories.body: Teachers and doctors in Catalunya are converging on the Parlament building in Barcelona on Friday in what organizers hope will be a massive joint demonstration. Teachers are in their fourth day of rolling regional strikes, with the education department reporting 35% participation in Thursday's walkout across the Valles, Maresme, and Girona regions. Strikers occupied government education offices in Girona and Granollers. Doctors are also striking again over working conditions, with rural areas reporting days without any physician available. The unions are pushing back against a salary agreement signed by the Generalitat with CCOO and UGT, which the majority teaching unions Ustec and Aspepc rejected, seeking conditions similar to the February 11 strike that emptied classrooms and brought 70,000 teachers into the streets.

stories.headline: Body of missing American student Jimmy Gracey found in Barcelona waters
stories.body: Police divers recovered the body of James "Jimmy" Gracey, a 20-year-old University of Alabama student from the Chicago suburb of Elmhurst, in the waters near Barcelona's Port Olympic at four meters depth. Gracey had been missing since separating from friends around 3am Tuesday outside the Shoko nightclub on the city's waterfront during a spring break trip. His wallet was found floating near the beach on Wednesday, and police later recovered his phone from a repeat offender who claimed to have found it on the sand. Investigators are treating the death as an accidental drowning, with no criminal motive suspected. The body was transferred for autopsy. The case received extensive coverage in US media, where family members had been making public appeals for information.

stories.headline: Barcelona invests 22 million euros to revitalize Avinguda Parallel
stories.body: Barcelona city hall unveiled a 22-million-euro plan to revitalize the iconic Avinguda Parallel, the two-kilometer avenue stretching from Drassanes to Placa Espanya historically known for its theaters. The plan, running from 2026 to 2028, includes 22 interventions organized around three axes: artistic and commercial revitalization including a new brand identity for the avenue, cultural reactivation centered on reopening the Teatre Arnau in 2027 and integrating the street into major city events like Sant Jordi and La Merce, and 11.6 million euros in urban transformation including renovated sidewalks, new lighting, reforms to Placa Bella Dorita and Placa dels Ocellets, and the transformation of the Tres Xemeneies square.

stories.headline: Catalan Parliament approves tram connection through Diagonal, pending budget
stories.body: The long-debated project to connect Barcelona's two tram networks through the Avinguda Diagonal received definitive approval, though its execution remains contingent on budget availability. The connection would unify the Trambaix and Trambesos systems that currently terminate at opposite ends of the Diagonal, a project that has been discussed for over a decade. The approval comes as the Catalan government approved a supplementary credit of nearly 6 billion euros to keep functioning without formal budgets.
